data.title: Synopsys Bets on AI-Enabled Ecosystem to Power Automotive Digital Twins
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

At embedded world 2026, one trend was made abundantly clear: AI-enabled ecosystems are heralding in a new dawn in the software-defined era. A case in point is Synopsys, which is employing Synopsys and ecosystem partner toolsets combined with AI technologies to enhance its latest Electronics Digital Twin (eDT) platform, built to enable automotive OEMs to complete up to 90% of software validation before the hardware becomes available.

“Automotive engineering teams are at their breaking point with more than 600 million lines of software, hundreds of software suppliers, rapidly shrinking development cycles, and mounting cost pressures,” said Ravi Subramanian, chief product management officer at Synopsys, in the company’s press release.

Synopsys has developed the eDT platform to reduce automotive OEMs’ development time and costs through its open ecosystem where companies can utilize digital twin technologies with pre‑integrated Synopsys and partner tools. These include Arm IP models and Synopsys VDKs for Infineon’s AURIX family, MIPS’ physical AI core models, NXP’s S32x processor families, Renesas’ RH850 and R-Car family, and STMicro’s Stellar family—to name a few.

“With the new eDT platform, Synopsys is transforming engineering with an end‑to‑end digital twin foundation, bringing together our product and market leadership supplying virtual SoC models and large‑scale system simulations, along with our extensive partner ecosystem, to simplify, accelerate, and scale the development of next‑generation vehicles,” Subramanian said.

Digital twins: the great equalizer

As physical AI becomes ever more pervasive, hardware validation must keep pace. Waiting for a physical prototype means developers and manufacturers risk falling behind, increasing time-to-market and, in turn, incurring higher costs.

Digital twin technology aims to marry the physical and digital worlds together, changing the development of physical AI from a tightly coupled process to a more open, collaborative one.

In an interview with EE Times, Synopsys’ VP of product Marc Serughetti said, “The way [physical AI systems] were developed in the past was very monolithic… You were waiting for test benches to be available. Rather than waiting for those physical test benches, you must shift left by virtualizing.”

Digital twins are by no means a new concept. What differentiates Synopsys’ platform from other solutions in the market, however, is its use of a fully integrated ecosystem of AI-powered products to test and validate software prior to hardware availability. This means assigning mundane coding tasks to AI tools, such as simulation, testing, and debugging, with little intervention needed from the developer, reducing processes that often take months to just a few weeks or even minutes, the company said.

Imagine being able to design a realistic digital twin of a fully functional product and receiving test benches that help identify software issues without having to physically build and test the solution—the benefits of such an approach speak for themselves.

That’s the concept behind Synopsys’ eDT platform. But how do you make such a platform accessible to a wide range of companies, each with its own workflows? The answer lies in the cloud.

Scalable compute abets collaboration

Synopsys describes its eDT platform as an open solution, citing its cloud-based environments and a set of pre-integrated assets that include Synopsys technologies, partner tools, models, and software.

“The open platform means that we want everybody to participate, [we want] everybody to provide their content, and that means you have to have an open API,” Serughetti told EE Times. “The way we see it, people are going to have their own ‘bring your own cloud’ approach, so whether they rely on Microsoft or Google, we don’t care…it’s completely open.”

In particular, the eDT platform utilizes an open-source SIL kit developed in collaboration with Vector, which enables teams “to rapidly assemble and connect virtual ECUs, models, and software components,” Synopsys said. This, in turn, allows automotive OEMs to essentially configure cloud-based eDT labs, gaining access to early virtual prototypes and testing.

“Software-defined vehicles are reshaping how automotive systems are designed, validated, and operated. As software and AI become the dominant value drivers, the industry must move toward scalable, platform-based development approaches,” said Gavin Rogers, senior VP at Vector, in Synopsys’ press release. “By combining Synopsys’ electronics digital twin platform with Vector’s automotive-proven software platforms and software factory, we are jointly enabling a seamless, software-first development workflow across the entire vehicle lifecycle.”

Whether Synopsys’ approach becomes the dominant model remains to be seen. But the direction is clear: As software complexity continues to rise, the automotive industry is looking for ways to validate systems earlier, faster, and at scale. Digital twins, combined with AI-driven automation, could play a central role in making that possible.

data.title: One Billion Cellular IoT LPWAN Connections
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

According to the GSMA, by the end of 2025, the global telecommunications sector reached 1 billion active NB-IoT and LTE-M low-power wide area network (LPWAN) connections. This milestone crowns a decade of strategic collaboration among mobile operators, hardware vendors, and standards organizations to build a unified, interoperable foundation for massive machine-type communications.

“Reaching 1 billion low-power IoT connections is a testament to what sustained industry collaboration can achieve,” said Alex Sinclair, CTO of GSMA. “This milestone reflects a shared commitment to standards, interoperability, and long-term value—and it lays the foundation for the next phase of massive IoT growth.”

By reaching this benchmark, cellular IoT has matured from an early concept into a globally deployable, highly scalable commercial platform.

A decade ago, the industry was fractured by competing methods and inconsistent implementations, sharply limiting enterprises’ capacity to scale connected device rollouts. Industry consensus on cellular LPWAN gave developers and operators the confidence to pursue and sustain long-term global deployments.

Synergy of NB-IoT and LTE-M

The cellular IoT ecosystem is anchored by two complementary technologies: narrowband IoT (NB-IoT) and LTE category M1 (LTE-M). These were the first cellular networks designed for IoT. The 3GPP formally introduced them in Release 13 as part of the 4G standard.

Both network standards use licensed spectrum to offer high reliability, extended coverage, and long battery life. They also support low-cost hardware modules that are ready for future network upgrades.

However, the two technologies serve different operational needs. NB-IoT is optimized for stationary sensors in hard-to-reach areas, such as deep indoors, underground, or over large fields. These sensors transmit small, simple data payloads.

In contrast, LTE-M serves mobile devices and sensors that need higher data speeds and handle larger payloads. It also supports SMS and limited voice transmissions. Both can get regular over-the-air firmware updates.

Strategic transition to 5G eRedCap

As telecommunications providers prepare to retire legacy 2G and 4G infrastructure, the industry is transitioning toward 5G networks, with enhanced Reduced Capability (eRedCap) technology emerging as the new standard for long-lifecycle IoT devices.

ERedCap, based on 3GPP Release 18, occupies the middle ground between advanced 5G and ultra-low-power narrowband IoT. It offers the higher throughput required for data-intensive applications, such as industrial automation, advanced wearables, and AI-driven consumer devices. It does so at lower cost and with less power consumption than a full 5G New Radio.

“The future of IoT is clearly converging around eRedCap,” said Nohik Semel, CEO of Sony Semiconductor Israel. “It brings the performance needed for tomorrow’s connected devices while preserving the efficiency and longevity that industries count on. For device makers designing for the next 10 to 20 years, eRedCap is the technology that will facilitate the move from 4G into a fully 5G world.”

Adopting eRedCap with half-duplex frequency division duplexing (HD-FDD) cuts radio frequency complexity and further lowers power consumption for cost-sensitive assets.

Market analysts project the eRedCap market could surpass 50 million unit shipments by 2030, as the demand for real-time edge connectivity and the eventual sunsetting of 4G networks drives operators to unify their networks and reallocate scarce spectrum resources.

From eSIM to iSIM

Traditional removable SIM cards limit enterprise IoT deployments, as managing, tracking, and physically replacing legacy SIM cards across thousands or millions of distributed assets imposes logistical and economic barriers.

Operators continue to struggle with shutting down legacy 2G networks because many connected devices, including smart meters, alarm systems, and security access points, still depend on them.

Physical SIM cards add size, are susceptible to environmental damage, and may be stolen, creating security risks. ESIM solves these by being permanently soldered into the device. This shrinks the device’s footprint to a fraction of a Nano SIM.

However, the integrated SIM (iSIM) fundamentally shifts device architecture by embedding SIM functionality directly into a dedicated, secure enclave on the device’s main SoC, allowing it to operate alongside the primary processor and cellular modem.

This integration makes the iSIM 98% smaller than a standard eSIM, freeing silicon for manufacturers designing highly compact devices. Eliminating a separate chip can slash standby power use by up to 70%, significantly lengthening battery life for field assets.

From a manufacturing perspective, iSIM integrates the microcontroller, radio transceiver, and SIM operating system into a single hardware component, reducing the overall bill of materials from three parts to one. Market forecasts indicate that 7 billion iSIM-enabled devices will ship between 2021 and 2030, highlighting their rise.

Over-the-air onboarding and the SGP.32 standard

Remote SIM Provisioning unlocks the full potential of eSIM and iSIM hardware, enabling enterprises to update network operator profiles over-the-air without manual intervention.

Historically, the GSMA established two primary specifications: SGP.02 for machine-to-machine communications and SGP.22 for consumer devices. The earlier machine-to-machine standard relied heavily on SMS routing and complex server integrations, creating a rigid, vendor-locked architecture that required pre-deployment network agreements and hindered dynamic global mobility.

The consumer framework addressed these challenges through a user-facing interface, but it required human input, which is not feasible for headless IoT sensors in remote locations.

In 2023, the GSMA introduced the SGP.32 standard specifically for IoT devices to bridge the gap between previous standards, enabling bulk remote provisioning of SIM profiles without SMS dependencies or direct user interaction.

A key part of the SGP.32 system is the eSIM IoT Remote Manager. It lets operators remotely enable, disable, delete, and download network profiles for thousands of devices at once. The IoT Profile Assistant receives commands and updates the chip directly.

Profile State Management Operations use cryptographic authentication to ensure that only authorized entities can remotely modify the device’s connectivity status. This overall capability enables enterprises to dynamically switch connectivity providers post-deployment, optimize network costs, and avoid restrictive operator lock-ins.

Global coverage, security, and production

For enterprise IoT, consistent global reach and data security are non-negotiable. Growing LTE-M and NB-IoT roaming agreements enable devices to operate seamlessly across nations without requiring regional hardware changes or complex multi-SIM setups.

One clear example is the eSIM present in most recent vehicles. In Europe, all new passenger vehicles and trucks are currently required to have a cellular system for emergency assistance (eCall). Automakers rely on eSIMs to meet the requirement, allowing the end user to choose the operator in their market. Then, the service credentials are transmitted to the eSIM, activating the  service.

From a security standpoint, both eSIM and iSIM serve as standardized hardware root of trust, authenticating the device to the cellular network and securing data transmissions to cloud infrastructure.

Adhering to standards like IoT SAFE ensures end-to-end data confidentiality, which is crucial as enterprises depend on sensor data to power AI models and automate logistics.

By following stringent compliance processes, such as the GSMA Security Accreditation Scheme, the ecosystem ensures that manufacturing, profile generation, and subscription management operations meet rigorous international security standards.

As device volumes scale, manufacturers have modernized their manufacturing processes to maintain efficiency. The GSMA SGP.41 and SGP.42 standards formalize in-factory profile provisioning, which permits original equipment manufacturers to securely load mobile network SIM profiles onto devices directly on the factory assembly line.

This approach enables manufacturers to produce a single global stock-keeping unit, customizing it with the appropriate regional network credentials just before shipping. By pre-configuring devices based on their destination, manufacturers ensure reliable out-of-the-box connectivity when devices power on in the field, while simultaneously reducing supply chain complexity and operational logistics costs.

By moving away from fragmented, proprietary technologies and adopting standardized protocols like NB-IoT, LTE-M, and the forthcoming 5G eRedCap, the telecommunications industry has established a highly stable platform for large-scale enterprise digitalization.

Upcoming 6G and satellite connectivity

The evolution of hardware from physical cards to highly integrated eSIM and iSIM architectures, combined with the flexible over-the-air onboarding capabilities of the SGP.32 standard, now effectively resolves the logistical and financial hurdles of global device management.

The upcoming 6G mobile networks will build on these technologies. Additionally, it will add cellular non-terrestrial networks (NTN) for true worldwide connectivity.

Together, these advancements ensure that the foundational infrastructure for massive machine-type communications is both scalable and highly secure.

See also:

Nokia 6G Horizon, AI, Integration, and Beyond

What Comes Next for Arduino after Qualcomm’s Acquisition?

Wienke Giezeman Shares Vision on Low Power Networks

Drones are a Key Part of the Smart Farming Revolution

LoRaWAN helps cities tackle new challenges

data.title: Taiwan’s Emerging Power Electronics Strategy in the AI Era
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Over the last three decades, Taiwan has built a world-class semiconductor industry cluster in advanced digital logic. From groundbreaking CMOS nodes to high-volume foundry manufacturing, wafers produced on the island have fueled the electronic industry from mobile phones to specialized chips for computing and AI.

Yet as electrification shapes the global industry and energy efficiency becomes a core priority, leadership is no longer based solely on relentlessly reducing transistor size. It now depends on how efficiently electrical power is generated, transmitted, distributed, and managed across systems.

The widening reach of power electronics

Power electronics (PE) was long overshadowed by digital logic, a dichotomy that has been completely upended as PE is emerging as a pivotal and transformative technology. Electric vehicles, energy systems, smart industry, and AI-driven data centers require substantial improvements in power efficiency and energy density, thermal management, and high-level integration.

This shift has accelerated the introduction of wide-bandgap (WBG) materials such as silicon carbide

(SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) and prompted designers to explore ultra-wide-bandgap semiconductors such as gallium oxide or even diamond. The pioneering technologies, overlapping traditional IGBTs and MOSFETs, significantly reduce losses, enabling more compact and efficient designs.

A change in perspective?

In this dynamic scenario, Taiwan is leveraging its consolidated technological savvy in foundry infrastructure and packaging to build a global PE ecosystem beyond leading-edge digital nodes.

Renowned power equipment manufacturers—Delta, Lite-on, AcBel, and Phihong—are now intersecting with semiconductor innovation, while foundries and outsourced semiconductor assembly and test (OSAT) companies focus on dedicated processes, heterogeneous integration, and advanced assembly.

The role of AI

The confluence of AI and PE is accelerating this strategic shift across the semiconductor industry. As AI and compute workloads scale at an unprecedented rate, data centers face rising power density and efficiency constraints, necessitating smarter, highly integrated solutions capable of supporting the rapid performance gains of modern computing hardware.

In this context, Taiwan’s well-established expertise in systems-in-package and module integration is instrumental in addressing this convergence, potentially leading to differentiated architectures that extend beyond component-based competition.

Power electronics is reshaping value chains

Recognizing how global megatrends point to higher system-level energy efficiency requirements, Taiwan’s industry leaders, proactively supported by government policies, are reshaping semiconductor strategies alongside long-standing objectives such as increased digital logic density and continued node scaling.

For green mobility and renewable energy, efficient power conversion frequently outweighs the importance of raw compute performance.

Unlike digital logic, where performance scaling is primarily driven through transistor miniaturization and architectural abstraction, power semiconductors are directly governed by physical constraints, such as current handling, voltage breakdown, power dissipation, switching behavior, and long-term reliability. As performance strongly depends on packaging and system integration, the PE value chain is broader and more distributed. This dynamic places Taiwan in a favorable position, given its strengths in OSAT, foundries, and electronic manufacturing services, making PE a natural extension of its logic semiconductor heritage.

Building a local power semiconductor ecosystem

Taiwan’s government, acknowledging the high economic and strategic stakes of PE, has explicitly positioned power devices as a national technology priority. Recent industrial policies and R&D initiatives have progressively strengthened next-generation semiconductors, namely SiC and GaN, as deliberate enablers of the green transition. In contrast to digital semiconductors, building a competitive power device ecosystem requires coordinated contributions across materials, epitaxy, device design, specialized manufacturing workflows, packaging, and reliability testing.

Government-led programs aim to cultivate a sovereign domestic capability, initially focusing on moderate-volume applications in which diversification, performance, integration, and reliability matter more than scale. These objectives rest on strategic pillars combining academia-industry consortia, pilot lines, ecosystem cooperation, and reduced import dependence.

Foundries possess the underlying know-how

Although Taiwanese foundries are best known for producing the world’s most advanced CMOS logic nodes, they also have extensive experience in mature technologies well-suited to power electronics. IGBTs, MOSFETs, and analog ICs are built with larger geometries, thicker epitaxial layers, and specialized process flows—domains where mature-node expertise provides a clear advantage.

It is no surprise that several domestic foundries have begun selectively investigating or adapting power-oriented specialty processes in response to market demand and policy priorities. These initiatives are typically application-oriented, alliance-driven, or exploratory in nature rather than the result of large-scale, vertically integrated power semiconductor strategies. A few illustrative examples follow:

▶ Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation (PSMC): PSMC represents one of the most tangible examples of early involvement. Through a public partnership, the company is supplying 8-inch GaN-on-silicon wafers to Navitas Semiconductor from its Taiwan fab. They target mid-voltage GaN discretes and power ICs—marketed under brands such as GaNFast and GaNSense—optimized for data centers, EV charging infrastructure, and photovoltaic installations. While this collaboration does not imply an explicit pivot of the company’s focus toward power devices, it demonstrates how Taiwan’s existing fabs can be redirected toward producing modern power semiconductors.

▶ United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC): UMC has not announced specific programs involving GaN or SiC, but its decades-long expertise in mature processes such as high-voltage CMOS and analog platforms makes the company a potential manufacturer for silicon MOSFETs, IGBTs, and power management chips. Any such evolution would likely be incremental and facilitated through local joint ventures.

▶ Vanguard International Semiconductor (VIS): Similar considerations apply to VIS. With a stable production footprint and a broad portfolio of standard technologies, the company could also emerge as a candidate for future PE opportunities as market conditions evolve.

▶ Taiwan-Asia Semiconductor Corporation (TASC): TASC and its subsidiary ProAsia Semiconductor have unveiled plans centered on SiC MOSFETs and rectifiers. These activities are generally framed as long-term capability building for automotive, industrial, and renewable energy applications. While volumes, timelines, and manufacturing roadmaps remain limited, the accumulation of design expertise strengthens their prospects for future market participation.

▶ Taiwan Semiconductor Co. Ltd. (TSC): TSC is a supplier of silicon power devices targeting primarily automotive and industrial markets. Although these products are largely commoditized, they provide Taiwan with valuable manufacturing experience and supply chain stability in power components, a prerequisite for more advanced developments.

▶ FastSiC Semiconductor: FastSiC Semiconductor, founded in 2019 and headquartered in Hsinchu, specializes in SiC power devices as a fabless design house. It aims to accelerate the transition from traditional silicon-based PE to SiC technology by offering high-performance, reliable, and user-friendly products—claimed as a drop-in replacement for silicon MOSFETs.

Taiwan is also home to a growing number of power design houses and discrete device makers, although their current scale and global reach remain modest compared with established international leaders.

Materials and substrates

Basic materials, especially in today’s geopolitical context, remain one of the most challenging aspects of power semiconductor development, particularly for SiC. Taiwan’s presence in this area is still at an early stage, but strategically significant:

▶ GeChi Compound Semiconductor (GCCS): GCCS belongs to a small group of Taiwanese firms advancing SiC crystal growth, ingots, and wafer fabrication at both 6-inch and emerging 8-inch scales. Although these initiatives currently trail global frontrunners, they underscore a strategic push toward domestic wafer supply security.

By addressing critical supply chain bottlenecks, GCCS aims to progressively mitigate its dependence on foreign vendors. GlobalWafers, one of the world’s largest suppliers of semiconductor wafers (3 inches to 12 inches) used by foundries, also contributes indirectly by supporting high-voltage silicon and GaN-on-silicon platforms, though WBG substrates remain a specialized domain.

▶ Taiwan Applied Crystal (TAC): Established in 2012, TAC has emerged as a local producer of SiC crystal substrates. The company has been developing in-house PVT crystal growth systems and scaling wafer sizes toward 8 inches, helping to build Taiwan’s semiconductor ecosystem.

▶ TAISIC Materials Corporation: Alongside GCCS and TAC, TAISIC Materials Corp. produces SiC substrates, including both N-type and semi-insulating SiC, serving the PE supply chain and attracting strategic investment from Hon Hai, the world’s leading EMS provider, widely known as Foxconn.

Packaging as a diversification lever in PE

Packaging is among Taiwan’s strongest competitive advantages and a key differentiator in PE, as it directly impacts power semiconductor performance. Modern high-frequency applications demand increasingly lower thermal resistance, low parasitic inductance, high-current capability, and long-term reliability under harsh operating conditions.

These requirements enable the local manufacturers to move beyond conventional discrete device encapsulation toward integrated power solutions, where innovation translates into system-level performance gains. Such integration is particularly beneficial to EV traction inverters, industrial motor drives, and AI power delivery modules, where even marginal efficiency increments and reduced form factors are critical.

The core competences of Taiwan’s OSAT players provide a strong foundation for: advanced SiC and GaN power modules; heterogeneous integration of power switches with sensors, controllers, and drivers; and high-performance substrates and thermal materials to build high-density systems.

ASE Group exemplifies these capabilities. As a global OSAT leader, ASE provides assembly services to many of the world’s largest power semiconductor companies, as a direct result of these differentiated OSAT competencies. These strengths often help achieve incremental performance improvements independent of the underlying semiconductors used, especially in efficiency-critical applications such as AI power supplies, smart factories, and electricity transportation.

Equipment suppliers as practical anchors

Delta Electronics, a world leader providing power system solutions, serves as a cornerstone of Taiwan’s PE ecosystem. Its global market coverage in energy management and power conversion offers a strategic vantage point for identifying emerging efficiency standards. While not a chipmaker, Delta’s adoption of SiC and GaN devices represents a natural pull for local partnerships on dedicated technology roadmaps. Any shift toward domestic procurement is likely to be gradual, guided by cost consideration and supply chain stability.

Powertech Technology Inc. (PTI), another successful OSAT player, provides turnkey services for chip probing, packaging, and testing. Its core strengths in advanced IC packaging, testing, and heterogeneous integration are increasingly relevant to PE, including:

▶ Fan-out and panel-level packaging to reduce parasitics and improve switching efficiency

▶ Multi-chip packaging integrating power devices, drivers, controllers, and sensors

▶ Thermal-aware package design enabling high power densities and longer device lifetimes

While PTI focuses largely on memory, logic, and computing, these technologies could be adapted to power management ICs and integrated power modules.

A prudent but effective trajectory

These examples show that Taiwan’s PE initiatives are driven more by an ecosystem evolution than by a decision to dominate the market outright. Attempting to replicate the scale of consolidated incumbents would be ineffective and strategically unwise, potentially leading to direct confrontation with regional competitors. Nor would it be realistic to reproduce the unique form of leadership achieved in advanced digital logic, where the TSMC model remains difficult to duplicate.

AI as an enabler of power architectures

PE stands to benefit significantly from the deployment of AI in two ways:

▶ First, AI infrastructure itself drives unprecedented power demand, pushing data centers toward extreme requirements for power density, efficiency, and reliability. This trend accelerates the need for advanced power conversion platforms, high-efficiency DC/DC converters, and intelligent power distribution.

▶ Second, the rise of machine learning, advanced simulation tools, intelligent algorithms, and predictive maintenance is transforming how power systems are designed and optimized. These capabilities shorten design cycles and improve converter performance across topology selection, thermal management, and reliability assessment.

Taiwan’s prowess in AI hardware and system integration looks well-suited to fully exploit this convergence. By enhancing the efficiency of power converters within data centers, AI can help reduce the amount of energy required to sustain its growth, creating a virtuous circle in which technological progress mitigates environmental impact. This synergy could become a defining characteristic of Taiwan’s next-generation semiconductor ecosystem.

Taiwan’s strategy for third-gen semiconductors

Policy briefings and committee reports from Taiwan’s Legislative Yuan identify third-generation semiconductors, particularly SiC and GaN, as strategic technologies within Taiwan’s industrial agenda. Priorities include accelerating domestic R&D, strengthening capabilities across materials, device design, and manufacturing, and deepening coordination between upstream suppliers and downstream system integrators. Rather than competing on scale, the emphasis is on enabling high-efficiency power electronics for EVs, renewable energy, advanced communications, and AI infrastructure.

This direction aligns with broader government funding efforts. The Executive Yuan has allocated NT$12.2 billion (US$386 million) to the semiconductor sector as part of its strategic industry budget for the coming year. Legislative documents also call for benchmarking against regional peers, such as Japan, South Korea, and China, while leveraging targeted public investment and industry-academia collaboration to build a sustainable, differentiated power semiconductor ecosystem.

The role of academia and research

Taiwan’s academic and research community is central to the island’s emerging PE ecosystem. Universities such as NTU, NTHU, NYCU, and NCKU provide extensive expertise in WBG materials, device physics, power module design, and thermal management. Academia Sinica and NARLabs offer advanced characterization, epitaxy research, and prototyping capabilities, while ITRI and the Taiwan Semiconductor Research Institute serve as conduits between research and industrial deployment.

Strategic implications

PE, indispensable to the green energy transition, is reaching a transformative inflection point within the semiconductor industry. Driven by electrification and the adoption of WBG materials, the market for power discretes and ICs is poised for sustained expansion. These materials offer superior performance in high-voltage and high-frequency environments, making them fundamental for EVs, renewable energy, and AI data centers.

By leveraging its world-class foundry and packaging ecosystem, Taiwan is set to actively steer this transition. However, securing a sustained leadership will require a cohesive strategy that integrates local design with specialized WBG manufacturing while wisely navigating regional competition and the industrial aspirations of neighboring economies.

data.title: AI Ushers In the Age of No-Compromise Gaming
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

As most gamers can attest, trading off performance in terms of frames per second (FPS) versus video quality is an all-too-common compromise that must be made. That is, until now. With industry leaders implementing AI into their super-resolution software, such as AMD’s FidelityFX Super Resolution (FSR) and Nvidia’s Deep Learning Super Sampling (DLSS), the days of having to choose between performance and quality while gaming are becoming a thing of the past, and with it, a new era of gaming is fast approaching. 

AMD and Nvidia lead the way

AMD recently released its latest edition of FSR software. Dubbed FSR Redstone, it builds upon the previously released FSR 4 and replaces its predecessor, FSR 3. FSR Redstone introduces some machine learning (ML) additions to its list of capabilities, with FSR Frame Generation and FSR Ray Regeneration being added to the already released FSR Upscaling. Additionally, AMD plans to implement FSR Radiance Caching sometime in early 2026.

Nvidia also released the latest version of its DLSS in January 2026. Its latest version, DLSS 4.5, provides several upgrades from the previous version, DLSS 4.0. Among these is a 6X mode for its multi-frame generation capability. This mode increases the number of AI-generated frames inserted between naturally rendered frames. In 4C mode, it inserts three frames, while in 6X mode it inserts five. DLSS 4.5 also features a dynamic multi-frame generation capability, which allows it to adapt how many frames (up to 6X mode) it inserts based on graphics intensity while still maintaining latency.

Effects on gaming

Currently, FSR Redstone has three main ML capabilities: upscaling, frame generation, and ray regeneration. Meanwhile, DLSS 4.5 features its own upscaling abilities, along with the previously mentioned 6X frame generation and dynamic frame generation.

Even though these capabilities are listed, the question is, what do these features do?

Upscaling is a process that takes lower resolution frames, for example, 1080p, and upscales them to a higher resolution, typically 4K. In doing so, upscaling allows for in-game performance similar to running the game at a lower resolution, while having the enhanced graphics quality found at a higher resolution. This is especially valuable in first-person shooter games, allowing the gamer to leverage the optimum FPS for click-to-pixel responsiveness while also utilizing higher resolution. In these types of games, the higher resolution isn’t just about aesthetics; it improves the immersive experience by enhancing the gamer’s ability to identify threats or opponent locations.

Frame generation, which both FSR and DLSS offer as well, uses AI to insert artificial frames between naturally rendered frames. This process enables a higher number of frames to be generated per second, allowing for smoother and higher-quality gameplay without increasing the GPU load, thus enabling higher performance without the commensurate increase in power consumption.

FSR’s ray regeneration enhances ray tracing, a feature designed to make in-game graphics more realistic. Ray regeneration cleans up the inputs from the ray tracing, enhancing the visual quality that is produced and also lightening the GPU rendering load.

Tirias Research ran tests across multiple titles to confirm the performance gains claimed by AMD, along with the increase in graphics quality. Using AMD’s FSR Redstone software, average frames per second were tested at native 4K resolution, then with both the quality and performance modes of FSR’s upscaling turned on, and finally with frame generation enabled in those modes. Below are results from two of the games we tested, Cyberpunk 2077 and Marvel Rivals, both set to the highest graphics settings available.

Table 1: TIRIAS Research test results of AMD FSR Redstone performance.

Future of gaming

Our test results indicate that the use of AI enables no-compromise gaming experiences by delivering both performance and quality without the typical tradeoffs. The next step will be extending these technologies into spatial computing, such as virtual reality and mixed reality, as the gaming industry itself is doing. Currently, FSR and DLSS are primarily limited to PC gaming, but what if they can be implemented in spatial computing devices, as well?

The possibility of a fully immersive gaming experience, where the lines between the digital and real world are blurred to such a degree as to render it indistinguishable, becomes more probable. These super-resolution capabilities are the first step to achieving this future. When this happens, Star Trek’s holodeck might just cross the threshold from science fiction to reality.

data.title: Synopsys Shows Off First Synopsys-Ansys Products
data.description:

SANTA CLARA, Calif. — At Synopsys Converge, the EDA giant unveiled the first results of its Ansys acquisition last summer, demonstrating how Ansys’ simulation tools will work with Synopsys tools and workflows to combine multi-physics design with silicon design from a single platform.

“When we closed the acquisition of Ansys, our customers’ first question was: When do I get the technology?” Synopsys CEO Sassine Ghazi said during his keynote at the event. “We promised the first half of ’26 because we know the team—we’ve had a partnership since 2017. We know customers’ requirements very well because we work closely with them, but to deliver on that fused technology in that timeframe was a fantastic execution from our team.”

Combined co-design

The new combined company is investing in three key areas, Ghazi said: co-design, digital twins, and agentic AI.

“We’ve been doing co-design and digital twins for decades, true—the concepts are not new,” he said. “But engineers don’t make an investment in their workflow just because; you do it because you have constraints to deliver. For future products, the constraints are massive.”

For a robotics system, this could mean satisfying electronic, mechanical, thermal, and fluidic constraints simultaneously, but tighter constraints lead to innovation, Ghazi said, since they require engineers to think outside the box.

Synopsys thinks of co-design both vertically (for example, within electronics subsystems) and horizontally (for example, across mechanical and electronic subsystems). Vertical co-design has been the norm for some time in silicon design, Ghazi said, but horizontal co-design is now needed. The complexity of designs is leading to over-designing, where handing off between different domains requires design margins, which stack up. The key is how to reduce this margin, he said.

“In the AI superchips era, it’s no longer a vertical optimization, it’s a horizontal co-design,” he said. “[We need to] take into account thermal, warpage, the cracking of the dies, the mechanical aspect—and if you do it too late—think about the cost.”

The first combination of Synopsys and Ansys technologies will be called Multiphysics Fusion technology, aimed at combining Ansys golden multiphysics engines into Synopsys EDA products to tackle electromagnetic, thermal, and mechanical effects alongside silicon design. In the first release, there will be multiphysics solutions for timing signoff, for multi-die, design closure, and analog design. These combinations are under beta test with customers today, Ghazi said.

As an example of a system requiring multiphysics design, Ghazi showed the world’s first HBM4 test chip (produced with a memory partner), using Synopsys IP to connect the logic die and memory stack.  

As well as Synopsys products hosting Ansys technology, there are some product lines where Ansys is the host, Ghazi said.

“We had the first major Ansys product release since the acquisition, the R1 release, and again, we did not miss a beat,” he said. “There were a lot of worries from customers. This is a massive acquisition, complex integration, and we did it incredibly well based on our commitment to both our team internally and understanding the customer requirements to deliver to it.”

The R1 is a unified Synopsys-Ansys workflow that combines products from the two portfolios for the first time for tasks such as safety analysis, materials discovery and development, manufacturing process improvement, photonic design and optical simulation, and test automation software.

Digital twinning

While the concept of co-design has been around for years, design complexity is driving the need for digital twins both at the silicon level and at the system level, at multiple levels of abstraction, Ghazi said. Physical protoyping is getting harder, to the point where it will become impossible for intelligent systems, he said.

Applications like automotive need digital twins of the physical product, of the electronics, and of the environment—all are separate engineering efforts, which means an ecosystem is needed to deliver.

Ghazi announced eDT (electronic digital twins), an open platform for digital twins based in the cloud, which can plug into ecosystem efforts for digital twins in different domains. It will initially focus on automotive use cases.

“Think of it as the operating system you need to design a digital twin for an autonomous vehicle,” Ghazi said. “This is the modern way of providing software-defined hardware-assisted verification to our customers.”

The company has already partnered with Nvidia on Omniverse for digital twins of the environment. Ansys Fluent (computational fluid dynamics product) and Ansys AV Accelerate (for autonomous vehicles) will accelerate the bring-up of other domains’ digital twins inside Omniverse.

Agentic AI

“When I talk to customers about agentic AI, at the user level, there is still a mix of excitement and fear,” Ghazi said. “The fear is around how will it change my job? The excitement is [around] a huge productivity booster. As you go up the organizational chain, there’s a lot of excitement.”

Higher-level managers see the opportunities for agentic AI, he said, since they understand that the number of engineers they have is the limiting factor most of the time.

Synopsys is making progress up the levels of autonomy for agentic AI, he said. Six Synopsys co-pilot agents are available at L1, which can generate outcomes rather than the user doing it manually. There are 24 Synopsys task agents at L2, whose role is to deliver on specific tasks assigned by human engineers. At L3, Synopsys has three multi-agent workflows with an orchestration layer, which means agents can manage each other. L4 and L5 require contextual awareness of multiple agents; an intelligent system based on a reasoning agent will dynamically orchestrate other agents.

Synopsys announced its first L4 agentic workflow at Converge. This workflow can, for example, handle architectural spec to RTL, build test plans, and handle formal verification, static verification, coverage, and debug with separate task agents.

“We’ve made tremendous progress here,” Ghazi said. “My urgency to you is [to] explore, be open-minded. I know scepticism is always good, but not good to a point that not taking advantage of what’s possible and the speed at which things are moving.”

Customers can also plug in their own agents into the workflow, he said.

Nvidia endorsement

Special guest, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, joined Ghazi on stage at the end of the keynote to add context to all three of Ghazi’s key announcements.

Reinventing computer graphics multiple times, as Nvidia has done, certainly requires a level of co-design, Huang said.

“Now we want to stack co-design from chips all the way out to the system, and now multi-systems, because in order for us to do distributed computing at the scale that we do, the computing fabric is inclusive of the CPU, the GPU, the scale-up switch, the scale-out switch, and the network processors are all part of the software stack,” Huang said.

The next step will be computers the size of a building, such that the building is part of the system, in a gigawatt data center. 

“We have to design the whole thing and refactor and redesign our algorithms all at the same time,” he said. “We work from the top-down, bottom-up, inside-out, outside-in, all at the same time. That’s extreme co-design.”

Test benches for physical AI need to be representative of the physical world and obey the laws of physics, which is “incredibly hard,” Huang said, especially with both software and hardware in the loop, mixing algorithms with AI and agents, and doing this for multiple robots at the same time.

“Omniverse is one of the most complex software systems the world’s ever made, and it took us almost a decade to get here,” he said.

Huang also stressed the importance of AI agents in the design process.

“This is the thing that almost every single analyst gets wrong,” Huang said. “The limitation of Nvidia is not anything except for the number of engineers we have. And that’s the reason why we’re constantly hiring more engineers.”

Every human Nvidia engineer will be given multiple Synopsys agents, specialized in different parts of the design phase, to collaborate with, he said. 

“I was in the first generation of engineers that were able to use tools to design chips rather than just using schematics and doing it by hand,” Huang said. “The last generation of engineers before me never believed it, but the generation of engineers now, after me, can’t imagine living without it.”

Tomorrow’s engineers will rely on extreme co-design, digital twins, and agentic AI.

“We’re in a new phase,” Huang said.
